title: WebSockets
summary: A WebSocket keeps a live two way connection open.
The server can send updates without being asked.
It is good for chat, live scores, and notifications.

sections.text: Normal requests are one and done, you ask, the server answers, the line closes. But some features need the server to push new data the moment it happens, like a chat message arriving. WebSockets keep a live line open for exactly that.

sections.title: A Live, Two Way Line
sections.text: A WebSocket is a connection that stays open between the browser and the server. Unlike a normal request that closes after one reply, this line stays connected, and either side can send a message at any time. The server can push new data to you without you asking again.

sections.title: When You Need It
sections.text: This matters for anything live. A chat app, live sports scores, a collaborative document, or instant notifications all need the server to reach the user the moment something changes. Repeatedly asking would be slow and wasteful. A WebSocket lets the update come to you instantly.

media.alt: An open WebSocket line with messages flowing both ways

sections.title: A Phone Call vs Sending Letters
sections.text: Normal requests are like sending letters, one message each way, then you wait and send another. A WebSocket is like a phone call left open, both people can speak the instant they have something to say, with no waiting to send a new letter each time.

media.alt: Mailed letters going back and forth beside an open phone call

sections.title: How a WebSocket Works
items: The browser opens a WebSocket connection to the server.
The connection stays open, not closing after one reply.
Either side can send a message at any time.
The server pushes updates the moment they happen.
You use this for live features like chat.

sections.text: Before WebSockets, sites faked live updates by asking the server over and over, very fast. It worked but wasted effort. WebSockets replaced that polling with a real open line.

terms.definition: A live two way connection that stays open.
The open line between browser and server.
Either side can send at any time.
The server sending data without being asked.
Updates that arrive the moment they happen.

questions.text: What does a WebSocket keep open?
questions.explanation: A WebSocket keeps a live two way connection open between browser and server.

questions.text: What can the server do with it?
questions.explanation: With a WebSocket, the server can push updates without being asked.
WebSockets are good for live features like chat, scores, and notifications.
